First, I looked for a single rule that applies consistently across all three pairs. I'm focusing on how shapes move or change. I started by noticing that some shapes seem to be replaced, and there are new shapes (all 3x3 squares) in the output. I also noticed how shapes with the same color are handled together. So I decided that I should handle the transformations by color.

In trying to determine the transformation, I spent considerable time determining if I could use bounding boxes. The bounding box of the large object became the anchor for the transformation of the small objects. I had a hypothesis where the new position would be based on the location of the smaller shape, and what the position of the large was relative to that. This line of thought failed.

I looked at all kinds of rules. Reflection, rotation, mapping from small to the large, different types of transformations that did not work. I also considered properties of the bounding boxes such as width.

I spent most of my time trying to find a consistent pattern. The one that was clear was that the 'r' coordinate was changed by +1 or -1 based on the location of the "large" object for that color. And I did find the rule that I used for columns.

The rule: For each color, the largest object (by area, then top-left tie-break) is unchanged. Each smaller object is replaced by a 3x3 solid square of the same color. The top-left corner (r, c) of the smaller object's bounding box is moved to a new position (r', c'). The new row r' is r+1 if the largest object's center is in the top half of the grid (row < 5), and r-1 otherwise. The new column c' is c plus the width of the largest object's bounding box.

This process highlights the intricacies of pattern recognition. It underscores the challenges of working from visual data.

If the new square overlaps with the largest component, the new square's color takes precedence.
